title: Cat Squinting and Pawing at Eye: Causes and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Is your cat squinting or pawing at their eye? Learn common causes, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage and care.
content_html:
What Is It?

When a cat squints or paws at their eye, it is technically known as blepharospasm. This behavior is a reflexive reaction to pain or irritation within the ocular structures. It often indicates that the cornea—the clear outer layer of the eye—or the sensitive tissues of the eyelid are inflamed. Common underlying issues include corneal ulcers (scratches), feline herpesvirus, conjunctivitis, or the presence of a foreign object trapped under the eyelid.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for eye discomfort in cats is Medium. While your cat may not be in a life-threatening situation, eye issues can progress rapidly and lead to permanent vision loss if left untreated. You should aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 12 to 24 hours. Seek immediate care if you notice the eye appears to be bulging, if the pupil size is uneven, or if the eye has turned a cloudy or milky color.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

In a triage setting, a high-quality photo is an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. Because cats often squint more tightly when stressed at the clinic, a photo taken in a calm environment allows the vet to see the eye's natural state. It helps identify the color and consistency of any discharge, the degree of redness, and the presence of visible scratches or swelling, ensuring your pet receives the correct priority level for an appointment.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for my cat's eye irritation?
faqs.answer: No. Never apply human medications, including over-the-counter redness relievers, to your cat's eyes. Many human eye drops contain chemicals that can be toxic or painful for felines.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat feel comfortable before the vet visit?
faqs.answer: If your cat is pawing aggressively, you can use an Elizabethan collar (cone) to prevent them from scratching the eye and causing further damage. You can also gently wipe away crusty discharge using a soft cloth and lukewarm water.

faqs.question: What if my cat is squinting but there is no discharge?
faqs.answer: Squinting without discharge still indicates pain. It could be a sign of early-stage glaucoma, uveitis (internal inflammation), or a very small corneal scratch that hasn't become infected yet.
